front: Ballet: commissioned with music by Stravinsky and choreography by Nijinsky; caused a riot/near-riot at its 1913 Paris premiere
back: The Rite of Spring (Le Sacre du printemps, 1913) (Diaghilev / Ballets Russes)
work: The Rite of Spring (Le Sacre du printemps, 1913)

front: Ballet: depicts the sacrifice of a virgin / "Chosen One" dancing herself to death in Bronze Age / pagan Russia
back: The Rite of Spring (Le Sacre du printemps, 1913) (Diaghilev / Ballets Russes)
work: The Rite of Spring (Le Sacre du printemps, 1913)

front: Ballet: Nicholas Roerich designed long-braided ancient Slavic folk costumes for this 1913 succes de scandale
back: The Rite of Spring (Le Sacre du printemps, 1913) (Diaghilev / Ballets Russes)
work: The Rite of Spring (Le Sacre du printemps, 1913)

front: Ballet: divided into "The Adoration of the Earth" and "The Sacrifice"
back: The Rite of Spring (Le Sacre du printemps, 1913) (Diaghilev / Ballets Russes)
work: The Rite of Spring (Le Sacre du printemps, 1913)

front: Ballet: opens with a famous high bassoon solo, prompting Saint-Saens to say "if that's a bassoon, I'm a baboon"
back: The Rite of Spring (Le Sacre du printemps, 1913) (Diaghilev / Ballets Russes)
work: The Rite of Spring (Le Sacre du printemps, 1913)

front: Ballet: Diaghilev launched Stravinsky's career by hiring him to compose the music for this, Stravinsky's first ballet
back: The Firebird (L'oiseau de feu, 1910) (Diaghilev / Ballets Russes)

front: Ballet: Diaghilev commissioned it after being impressed by Stravinsky's Scherzo fantastique / Fireworks
back: The Firebird (L'oiseau de feu, 1910) (Diaghilev / Ballets Russes)

front: Ballet: Prince Ivan destroys an egg containing the soul of Koschei the Immortal using the title character's feather
back: The Firebird (L'oiseau de feu, 1910) (Diaghilev / Ballets Russes)

front: Ballet: features an "Infernal Dance" forced upon a group of soldiers
back: The Firebird (L'oiseau de feu, 1910) (Diaghilev / Ballets Russes)

front: Ballet: a 1926 Ballets Russes revival had costumes by Natalia Goncharova
back: The Firebird (L'oiseau de feu, 1910) (Diaghilev / Ballets Russes)

front: Ballet: a 1949 Balanchine production had costumes by Marc Chagall and was the breakout role of Maria Tallchief
back: The Firebird (L'oiseau de feu, 1910) (Diaghilev / Ballets Russes)

front: Ballet: Diaghilev assigned its choreography to Nijinsky instead of Fokine, causing Fokine to leave the Ballets Russes
back: The Afternoon of a Faun (L'Apres-midi d'un faune, 1912) (Diaghilev / Ballets Russes)
work: The Afternoon of a Faun (L'Apres-midi d'un faune, 1912)

front: Ballet: Nijinsky mimed masturbation with a nymph's scarf at the end of this ballet
back: The Afternoon of a Faun (L'Apres-midi d'un faune, 1912) (Diaghilev / Ballets Russes)
work: The Afternoon of a Faun (L'Apres-midi d'un faune, 1912)

front: Ballet: Le Figaro reviewers called its main character "incontinent, with vile movements of erotic bestiality"
back: The Afternoon of a Faun (L'Apres-midi d'un faune, 1912) (Diaghilev / Ballets Russes)
work: The Afternoon of a Faun (L'Apres-midi d'un faune, 1912)

front: Ballet: a 12-minute ballet at whose premiere Nijinsky made tremendous leaps Marie Rambert described as "near the stars"
back: The Afternoon of a Faun (L'Apres-midi d'un faune, 1912) (Diaghilev / Ballets Russes)
work: The Afternoon of a Faun (L'Apres-midi d'un faune, 1912)

front: Ballet: features a notorious tight unitard with brown patches meant to represent a faun
back: The Afternoon of a Faun (L'Apres-midi d'un faune, 1912) (Diaghilev / Ballets Russes)
work: The Afternoon of a Faun (L'Apres-midi d'un faune, 1912)

front: Ballet: a collaboration between Erik Satie (music), Jean Cocteau (libretto), and Pablo Picasso (Cubist sets/costumes), with Giacomo Balla helping on the curtains
Ballet: choreographed and danced at its 1917 premiere by Leonide Massine
Ballet: features three impresarios futilely trying to lure spectators into their theaters; the title refers to the side acts that become the focus
Ballet: Guillaume Apollinaire coined the term "Surrealism" in this ballet's program notes
Ballet: Picasso's costumes forced dancers to wear boxy outfits representing buildings
Ballet: the introverted title puppet "should be all turned in" while the Moor is "all turned out"; they vie for the love of the Ballerina
Ballet: Nijinsky was the first to dance the title role of this puppet ballet
Ballet: choreography by Bronislava Nijinska, the younger sister of Diaghilev's lover Nijinsky
Ballet: a Stravinsky ballet scored for mixed chorus, percussion, and four pianos, depicting a Russian peasant wedding
Ballet: includes the tableaus "The Bridegroom's House" and "The Wedding Feast"
Ballet: a plotless ballet blanc re-choreographed by Michel Fokine for the first Ballets Russes season, in which female spirits dance in the moonlight around a male "poet"
Ballet: its music uses the Military Polonaise and other works by Frederic Chopin, originally orchestrated by Glazunov
Ballet: renamed from its original title Chopiniana

Impresario: went through the painstaking process of choosing 20 of Domenico Scarlatti's 555 sonatas to score an adaptation of Carlo Goldoni's The Good-Humored Ladies — the first of what Constant Lambert termed his "time-travelling" works
Ballet: a neoclassical ballet whose music is based on compositions spuriously attributed to Giovanni Pergolesi
Impresario: commissioned La Boutique fantasque from Ottorino Respighi and Leonide Massine, with sets designed by Andre Derain
work: Other commissions (La Boutique fantasque, Le dieu bleu, Le Spectre de la rose, The Sleeping Princess, etc.)

front: Impresario: produced Le dieu bleu, with music by Venezuelan-born Reynaldo Hahn (lover of Marcel Proust)
work: Other commissions (La Boutique fantasque, Le dieu bleu, Le Spectre de la rose, The Sleeping Princess, etc.)

front: Ballet: a 1911 work that Michel Fokine choreographed to music of Carl Maria von Weber, ending with Nijinsky leaping out a window as a personified flower
back: Le Spectre de la rose (Diaghilev / Ballets Russes)
work: Other commissions (La Boutique fantasque, Le dieu bleu, Le Spectre de la rose, The Sleeping Princess, etc.)

front: Impresario: his 1921 London production of The Sleeping Beauty (under an altered title) flopped, drawing criticism that he was "frivolous about serious people"
work: Other commissions (La Boutique fantasque, Le dieu bleu, Le Spectre de la rose, The Sleeping Princess, etc.)

front: Impresario: commissioned Manuel de Falla to rewrite his pantomime The Magistrate and the Miller's Wife, resulting in The Three-Cornered Hat
work: Other commissions (La Boutique fantasque, Le dieu bleu, Le Spectre de la rose, The Sleeping Princess, etc.)

front: Impresario: commissioned Prokofiev's The Prodigal Son but rejected Prokofiev's Scythian Suite
work: Other commissions (La Boutique fantasque, Le dieu bleu, Le Spectre de la rose, The Sleeping Princess, etc.)

front: Impresario: commissioned Germaine Tailleferre's ballet La nouvelle Cythere about the discovery of Tahiti, never performed because his death cancelled the season
work: Other commissions (La Boutique fantasque, Le dieu bleu, Le Spectre de la rose, The Sleeping Princess, etc.)

front: Impresario: had a falling out with Ravel over La valse (refusing to choreograph it), prompting Ravel to challenge him to a duel
work: Other commissions (La Boutique fantasque, Le dieu bleu, Le Spectre de la rose, The Sleeping Princess, etc.)

front: Impresario: a flamboyantly gay impresario whose lovers included Nijinsky and (after firing Nijinsky) Leonide Massine
Impresario: fired Nijinsky after his loveless marriage to the Hungarian stalker Romola de Pulszky, replacing him with Massine, and supposedly "gave himself to a wild orgy of dissipation"
Impresario: co-founded the periodical Mir iskusstva (World of Art) with Alexandre Benois and Leon Bakst, who later designed his sets and costumes
Impresario: inspiration for Boris Lermontov in Powell and Pressburger's film The Red Shoes
Impresario: differences with Prince Volkonsky cancelled their planned collaborative staging of Sylvia
Impresario: wrote the article "More About Julius Caesar" praising a contemporary for adhering to the "producer-autocrat" model
Impresario: hired modernist painters like Picasso, Goncharova, Larionov, Sonia Delaunay, de Chirico, Derain, Miro, Chanel, and Matisse to design costumes and sets
Impresario: his company's dancers were largely recruited from the Saint Petersburg / Mariinsky / Imperial Ballet and adhered to Enrico Cecchetti's weekly schedule
Impresario: W. H. Auden's "September 1, 1939" quotes "What mad Nijinsky wrote / About [this man] / Is true of the normal heart" — the source of the title of Larry Kramer's play The Normal Heart
